codeReviewIssues.description: clear_buffer() uses unsafe ptr::write_bytes to zero a &mut [u8]. This is safe but unnecessary — buf.fill(0) is a safe alternative that the compiler optimizes to memset. The SAFETY comment is present and correct, but preferring the safe API would be more idiomatic.

end_frame() uses swap_remove which invalidates PoolHandle indices for entries that were swapped. Under the intended usage pattern (release all handles before end_frame), this is safe because only non-in_use entries are removed. However, if a caller ever calls end_frame while still holding a released handle AND later tries to use it (violating the contract), the handle would silently refer to the wrong entry or be out of bounds. A generation counter or stable ID scheme would make PoolHandle robust against misuse. Current tests cover the correct usage pattern, so this is a latent footgun rather than a live bug.

MAX_ENTRIES (32) caps the number of pooled buffers as a defensive measure, which is sound. However this limit isn't mentioned in the module doc comment or the feature description. Consider documenting the cap and its rationale (preventing metadata bloat) in the module-level doc so future consumers know the constraint.

sharedStateObservations.description: The SurfacePool API contract (release all handles before end_frame, handles are invalidated after end_frame due to swap_remove) is implicit. If other components use SurfacePool in the future, documenting this lifecycle contract in the architecture library could prevent misuse.

summary: Clean, well-structured implementation that covers all 7 feature requirements with 16 comprehensive tests. Correct budget enforcement, size-matched reuse, transparent clearing, and frame lifecycle management. Two non-blocking issues: unnecessary unsafe in clear_buffer (safe alternative exists) and a latent PoolHandle stability footgun from swap_remove in end_frame (safe under current usage pattern). No blocking issues.
